text: The surgeon demonstrates the width of the rectus muscle, which appears wide in this patient, while attempting to access the preperitoneal plane during a robotic right lower quadrant Spigelian hernia repair. A vertical indentation visible on the right side of the screen marks the linea semilunaris. The surgeon works to bring down the thin transversalis fascia.
The surgeon opens the posterior rectus sheath to access the retro-rectus space, transitioning from a preperitoneal approach to a retro-muscular technique for hernia repair.

The surgeon uses a grasper to approach the linea semilunaris laterally, avoiding disruption of this structure. The dissection continues in the retro-rectus space, where the fat remains against the rectus muscle, protecting the inferior gastric and neurovascular bundles. The retro-rectus dissection is visibly easier compared to the preperitoneal plane.

The surgeon is performing a dissection similar to an inguinal hernia repair, working within the retro-rectus space. The attenuated posterior rectus sheath is visible on the floor of the surgical field, indicating the surgeon's position within the retro-rectus space.

The surgeon divides the attenuated posterior rectus sheath, also referred to as the transverse salus fascia, to gain lateral space for mesh overlap. The linea semilunaris is visible as a wall, and the dissection continues medial to this structure.

Below the arcuate line, the surgeon identifies a thickened layer of attenuated posterior rectus sheath and creates a pocket behind it to push off the peritoneum. The posterior rectus sheath is divided, revealing two layers similar to those above the arcuate line. The surgeon carefully maneuvers around the hernia sac, with scissors moving preperitoneal under the layer and retrorectus above it.

The surgeon uses a grasper to peel the peritoneum off the posterior rectus sheath, aiming to divide the sheath vertically without cutting the peritoneum. This maneuver merges the medial retro-rectus space with the lateral pre-peritoneal space.

The surgeon dissects the preperitoneal space to separate the peritoneum from the abdominal wall. The remaining portion of the posterior rectus sheath is cut vertically, medial to the linea semilunaris, transitioning from the retrorectus space to the preperitoneal space without disrupting the linea semilunaris.

The surgeon navigates the retrorectal space, carefully respecting the linea alba and linea semilunaris, while transitioning into the preperitoneal space to create a large pocket for addressing a lower abdominal hernia.
